summary: Drew DeVault provides an updated fee calculator for Patreon's May 2019 fee model changes, helping creators and supporters understand how their money moves through the platform. He explains the different fee structures for founding creators versus new creators, the impact of charge-up-front options, and the various plan tiers. He raises pointed questions about Patreon's shift from batched to per-creator payment processing fees and where the extra money might be going. The post reflects his broader skepticism of VC-backed platforms controlling donation flows.
key_points: Patreon's fee model changed in May 2019, with founding creators grandfathered into lower rates while new creators face higher fees
The shift from batched to per-creator payment processing fees raises questions about where the extra revenue goes if Patreon still batches payments internally
As a VC-backed corporation, Patreon's fee structures are prone to change in ways that prioritize profit and infinite growth over creator welfare
DeVault advocates income diversification across platforms to limit the impact of any single platform's fee changes
money_quotes: Patreon makes money by siphoning some money off the top of a donation flow between supporters and creators.

Because of the nature of its business (a private, VC-backed corporation), the siphon's size and semantics are prone to change in undesirable ways, since VC's expect infinite growth and a private business generally puts profit first.
For this reason, I diversify my income, so that when Patreon makes these changes it limits their impact on my financial well-being.
When we take a look at our bank returns in the coming months, if Patreon is still batching payments internally… hmm, where is the extra money going?
We can guess which plan our creator uses by looking at the features they use on Patreon.
key_insight: VC-backed platforms like Patreon will inevitably adjust fee structures to maximize profit, making income diversification essential for creators who depend on donations.
